fast.summary: This paper reviews serverless LLM inference challenges, focusing on cold start latency caused by large model checkpoints and GPU initialization overhead. It analyzes ServerlessLLM and related approaches like Rainbowcake that reduce startup times through multi-tier checkpoint loading, live inference migration, and startup-time-optimized scheduling.
fast.key_idea: By staging LLM checkpoints across a memory hierarchy (GPU memory, CPU RAM, fast storage) and migrating live inference requests, cold start latency can be reduced 6-8x compared to naive serverless approaches.
fast.why_it_matters: Serverless LLM deployment promises cost efficiency and elastic scaling, but cold start latency has made it impractical for production use — solving this unlocks pay-per-use economics for LLM APIs without sacrificing response time SLAs.
possible_use_cases: Cost-efficient LLM API hosting where traffic is bursty or unpredictable
Multi-tenant cloud platforms needing to serve many fine-tuned model variants without keeping all loaded simultaneously
Edge or regional inference deployments where GPU resources are limited and must be shared across many infrequently-used models

deep.method_breakdown: ServerlessLLM addresses cold start latency in serverless LLM inference by implementing a multitier checkpoint loading system that leverages underutilized GPU memory and storage hierarchies (GPU VRAM → CPU RAM → NVMe → network) to stage model weights closer to compute before a request arrives. It introduces live inference migration, allowing in-flight requests to be transferred between nodes without dropping them, and a startup-time-optimized scheduler that selects deployment targets based on estimated initialization cost rather than simple load balancing. Together these reduce cold start overhead by 6–8x versus naive checkpoint-loading approaches.
deep.key_innovation: The core novelty is treating cold start as a multi-level prefetching and staging problem rather than a binary load/don't-load decision. Prior work either kept models hot in memory (expensive) or loaded from remote storage on demand (slow). ServerlessLLM's tiered loading exploits the observation that serverless clusters have abundant idle GPU memory and NVMe bandwidth between requests, using that slack to pre-position checkpoint shards closer to the GPU before they are needed — without committing full GPU memory until a request arrives.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is decoupling checkpoint residency from GPU allocation: model weights can live in CPU RAM or NVMe while the GPU is still serving other models, then be streamed in with high PCIe/NVMe bandwidth once a slot opens. This works because modern NVMe → CPU → GPU bandwidth (tens of GB/s) is far faster than wide-area network retrieval, so even a one-tier prefetch yields large latency reductions. The tiered approach also allows fine-grained eviction policies that balance memory cost against expected reuse.
deep.limitations: The system is primarily a review/survey paper built around ServerlessLLM, so empirical results are inherited from the original work and may not generalize across cloud providers with different storage topologies. The 6–8x improvement assumes sufficient idle NVMe/CPU memory on the same physical host, which breaks down under high cluster utilization. Live migration adds network bandwidth pressure and serialization overhead that could hurt tail latency for very large models. The startup-time-optimized scheduler requires accurate cold-start time estimates, which are difficult to obtain for heterogeneous hardware or quantized/adapter-augmented checkpoints.
interview_questions: Why does cold start latency become especially severe for LLMs compared to traditional serverless functions, and what makes it qualitatively different?
Explain the multitier checkpoint loading strategy in ServerlessLLM — what tiers are involved and how does the system decide which tier to load from?
How does live inference migration work, and what are the main trade-offs it introduces?
What is a startup-time-optimized scheduler, and how does it differ from a throughput- or latency-optimized scheduler in a standard serving system?
What future directions or unsolved problems remain in serverless LLM inference after ServerlessLLM?
interview_answers: Traditional serverless functions are small (MBs) and stateless, so cold start is dominated by process/container initialization (seconds). LLMs have checkpoints ranging from tens to hundreds of GB, so even with fast storage the I/O alone dominates — loading a 70B model from S3 can take minutes. Additionally, GPU driver and CUDA context initialization adds further overhead absent in CPU-only functions, making the problem both quantitatively and qualitatively harder.

The tiers are: GPU HBM (hot), CPU DRAM (warm), local NVMe SSD (cool), and remote object storage (cold). When a model is predicted to be needed soon, the scheduler proactively promotes its checkpoint up the hierarchy during idle periods. At request time the system loads from the highest (closest) available tier, using PCIe DMA for CPU→GPU transfer and NVMe DMA for SSD→GPU or SSD→CPU paths. Eviction follows a cost-aware LRU policy that accounts for reload cost, not just recency.

Live migration serializes the current KV cache and partial generation state of an in-flight request, streams it over the network to a destination node that already has (or is loading) the model, and resumes generation there. The trade-off is added latency for the migrated request due to serialization and network transfer, plus bandwidth consumption that competes with checkpoint prefetching. It is most beneficial when the source node needs to be reclaimed urgently and the destination already has the model warm.

A startup-time-optimized scheduler scores candidate nodes not by current GPU utilization but by estimated time-to-first-token for a new request, factoring in which checkpoint tier is resident on each node. A node with the model in CPU RAM scores better than an idle node that must fetch from S3, even if the latter has lower GPU utilization. This contrasts with throughput schedulers (maximize tokens/sec) or SLA-based latency schedulers (minimize p99 for active requests) — those ignore cold-start cost entirely.

Open problems include: efficient handling of LoRA/adapter variants where the base model is shared but adapters differ (partial-tier loading); accurate cold-start prediction for quantized or compiled (e.g., TensorRT) checkpoints; cross-provider portability since tiered storage topologies vary; scheduling under memory pressure when idle capacity shrinks; and co-design with speculative decoding or disaggregated prefill/decode architectures where the unit of migration is no longer a single GPU process.
